nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still forest streams, swamps, and pools with soft, acidic water, heavily vegetated and carpeted with leaf litter and submerged roots that provide shelter and spawning cover.

decorPreferences: driftwood and submerged roots providing caves and line-of-sight breaks
flat smooth stones or broad leaves as potential spawning sites

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of foods. Offer high-quality micro-pellets or small cichlid pellets as a staple, supplemented with frozen or live foods such as brine shrimp, daphnia, cyclops, and bloodworms. Small invertebrates and insect larvae make up a significant portion of the wild diet. Vegetable-based flakes or spirulina can round out nutrition.

goodWith: small tetras (e.g. neon tetras, ember tetras, Congo tetras)
other small peaceful West African species
non-aggressive dwarf cichlids of similar size
avoidWith: boisterous tankmates that will outcompete for food
very small fish or shrimp during spawning (heightened parental defence)

summary: The Thomasi Cichlid (Anomalochromis thomasi) is one of the few African cichlid species gentle enough for a community planted aquarium. Also known as the African Butterfly Cichlid, it stays small, tends to be peaceful toward dissimilar tankmates, and pairs form strong monogamous bonds, guarding their eggs and fry together.
careNotes: The Thomasi Cichlid (Anomalochromis thomasi), also sold as the African Butterfly Cichlid, is one of the few African cichlid species genuinely suited to a community planted aquarium. Unlike most cichlids from the continent, it remains small (5–7.5 cm), tends to be peaceful toward dissimilar tankmates, and tolerates the moderately soft, slightly acidic to neutral water typical of planted-tank setups. A 75 L tank with a 60 cm footprint is adequate for a pair, though a 100–120 L tank gives more room for a community of compatible dithers. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–12) is preferred; the species originates from forest streams in Sierra Leone, Liberia, and Guinea where water is tea-coloured and mineral-poor. Indian almond leaves and driftwood help recreate this chemistry and provide behavioural enrichment. A well-planted tank with multiple sight-line breaks reduces territorial tension, especially when the pair is guarding fry. Offering varied small foods — quality micro-pellets, frozen brine shrimp, daphnia, and cyclops — keeps them in condition and encourages natural colouring. The species is generally considered beginner-friendly given its hardiness, comparatively undemanding water requirements, and willingness to accept prepared foods.
breedingNotes: Anomalochromis thomasi is a biparental, monogamous substrate spawner. Established pairs clean a flat stone, broad leaf, or smooth surface and deposit a clutch of adhesive eggs that typically number 100–300. Both parents defend the eggs and subsequently the free-swimming fry with notable vigour — temporary separation of delicate tankmates may be advisable during brood care. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 26°C; fry become free-swimming within a further 4–5 days. Suitable first foods include infusoria, commercial fry foods, or finely crushed flake, progressing to baby brine shrimp nauplii as the fry grow. Parents typically continue guarding fry for several weeks. The monogamous pair bond is strong, and pairs are best not separated once established. The species generally spawns readily in well-maintained conditions without specialist intervention, making it among the easier cichlids to breed.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Anomalochromis thomasi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Sierra Leone, Liberia, Guinea), adult size (~7.5 cm), temperature (23–28°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (soft), habitat (slow forest streams with leaf litter), diet (omnivore — invertebrates and plant matter), compatibility (peaceful, community-suitable), and detailed breeding notes (biparental substrate spawner, monogamous pair bond, clutch 100–300 eggs, fry free-swimming in ~5 days).

additional.name: FishBase — Anomalochromis thomasi
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 7.5 cm TL), native distribution (Sierra Leone, Guinea, Liberia — coastal river drainages), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), temperature range, and diet (omnivore, invertebrates). Confirmed monotypic genus Anomalochromis.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — African Butterfly Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care overview confirming beginner-friendly status, community tank suitability, tank size recommendation (20+ gallons / ~75 L for a pair), feeding (accepts pellets, flake, frozen foods), peaceful temperament, and biparental spawning behaviour. Temperature range 23–28°C and pH 6.0–7.5 consistent with other sources.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Anomalochromis thomasi profile
additional.notes: Species profile confirming West African origin (Sierra Leone / Liberia), adult size ~7 cm, soft acidic to neutral water preference, community suitability, monogamous pair-bond behaviour, and substrate-spawning with biparental care. Notes that the species is atypically peaceful for an African cichlid.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Anomalochromis thomasi species entry
additional.notes: Hobbyist reference confirming monogamous pairing, biparental substrate spawning, soft slightly acidic water preference, small adult size, and planted community tank compatibility. Noted clutch sizes of 100–300 eggs and fry guarding duration of several weeks.

fish.breedingNotes: A biparental, monogamous substrate spawner. The pair cleans a flat surface and deposits 100–300 adhesive eggs, with both parents guarding eggs and fry. Eggs hatch in approximately 48–72 hours at 26°C and fry become free-swimming in around 5 days. Suitable first foods include infusoria or baby brine shrimp nauplii. Parental care continues for several weeks. The species generally spawns readily in well-maintained planted tanks without specialist intervention.
